original_abstract:
In nature, nonheme iron enzymes utilize dioxygen to generate high-spin iron(IV)=O intermediates that drive a diverse array of essential oxygenation reactions. While synthetic chemists have long endeavored to replicate this sophisticated reactivity, the enzyme-like activation of O₂ to form high-spin iron(IV)=O species has remained an elusive goal in laboratory settings. 

In this work, we present a metal–organic framework featuring iron(II) sites with a local coordination environment that mimics the active sites found in α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases. Upon exposure to O₂ at low temperatures, the framework successfully forms high-spin iron(IV)=O species. These reactive intermediates were comprehensively characterized using a suite of spectroscopic techniques, including in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform (DRIFTS), in situ and variable-field Mössbauer, Fe Kβ x-ray emission, and nuclear resonance vibrational spectroscopies. Beyond their characterization, we demonstrate that these framework-stabilized species are chemically potent, facilitating the catalytic oxygenation of cyclohexane and the stoichiometric conversion of ethane to ethanol in the presence of dioxygen.
humanized_abstract:
Unlike biological hemeproteins, nonheme iron enzymes catalyze the utilization of dioxygen to give the high-spin iron(IV)=O species that facilitates a range of critical oxidation processes. This mode of iron-mediated oxygenation reactivity has long been the focus of synthesis and bio-inspired studies, however the activation of O₂ to give the iron(IV)=O species similar to those found in catalytic centers in enzymes and iron-oxidizing enzymes, respectively, has not been possible in a synthesis-compatible manner.

Here we report on an iron-containing metal–organic framework (MOF) in which the iron(II) ions possess the same local coordination environment as in the enzyme α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases, catalytic centers of which are known to generate their reactive iron-oxo species in vivo only upon being reacted with O2 at elevated temperatures. Below 243 K, the MOF reacts with O2 at low pressure to yield high-spin iron(IV)=O species that were identified using in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform (DRIFTS), in situ and variable-field Mössbauer, and Fe Kβ x-ray emission and nuclear resonance vibrational spectroscopies. Notably, the substrate- and O2-binding sites within these materials facilitate catalytic oxidation of cyclohexane as well as the stoichiometric oxidation of ethane to ethanol using O2.

document.input:
